title: Frankenstein — The Created Body as the Golem
subtitle: The creation of the astral body without the integration of the daimon — Mary Shelley's anatomy of the work made wrong

summary.one_line: Mary Shelley's Frankenstein as the allegory of the astral body created without daimonic integration — the work made wrong, rejected, and the catastrophe that follows.
summary.scope: Covers the creation of the creature as the astral body made without the daimon; the creature's rejection as the separation from the daimon; the hunt across the ice as the descent into the frozen earth tattva; Walton's frame narrative as the witness; the creature's disappearance into darkness as the threshold not crossed.

sources.label: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley — Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus (1818)

body.text: Victor Frankenstein's creation of the creature is the making of an astral body — a form built from the dead materials of nature without the integration of the daimon (the animating principle from above). Victor assembles the body from 'the dissecting room and the slaughter-house' — the elements of the lower tattvas collected without the ordering principle of the śuddha path. The animation is purely mechanical: 'I infused a spark of being into the lifeless thing.' This is prāṇa without consciousness — the vital force directed without the guidance of the daimon. The result is a golem: a body that lives but has no soul.

Victor's immediate rejection of the creature — the horror that overcomes him when the yellow eye opens — is the soul's rejection of its own astral body when it is made without the daimon. Victor runs from his laboratory, falls into a feverish sleep, and dreams of Elizabeth turning into the corpse of his mother — the nightmare of the impure path: the forms of the tattvas (Elizabeth as śakti, mother as nature) appearing as death. The creature is not evil but abandoned — its subsequent violence is the revenge of the rejected astral body that has no guide. The daimon-less form becomes a demon.

The creature's eloquent self-narration (Volume II) is the voice of the impure tattvas describing their own predicament. The creature learns language by watching the DeLacey family from the hovel — the tattvas acquire the capacity to reflect the pure path through proximity. The creature's demand for a female companion is the demand for the śakti (the feminine counterpart) that would complete its isolated existence. Victor's destruction of the female creature is the refusal to integrate the śakti — the work remains incomplete, the polarization between puruṣa and prakṛti unresolved.

The hunt across the ice at the novel's conclusion is the descent into the Earth tattva (mūlādhāra) in its most frozen, contracted form. Victor chases the creature across the Arctic — a landscape of pure ice, the 'frozen earth' where nothing grows and nothing changes. This is kalā (limited agency) taken to its extreme: the contraction of consciousness into a single point of pursuit. Both Victor and the creature are trapped on the ice with no way forward and no way back — the dead end of the impure path pursued to its logical conclusion. The ice breaks beneath Victor's sled as the tattva dissolves beneath the one who has exhausted it.

Robert Walton's frame narrative is the witness consciousness — the sākṣin who receives the story and must transmit it. Walton is the counterpart to the Wedding-Guest of the Ancient Mariner: the explorer who has pushed too far into the impure path (the Arctic as the mūlādhāra) and is stopped by the encounter with Victor. Walton's letters to his sister are the transmission of the warning: 'Learn from my miseries.' Walton's decision to turn back ('I have consented to return') is the crucial moment — the witness who chooses survival over the pursuit of the contracted. His sister Margaret Saville is the receiver of the transmission — M.S. as Mary Shelley herself receiving her own work.

The creature's final disappearance into the darkness ('I shall ascend my funeral pile triumphantly and exult in the agony of the torturing flames') is the threshold not crossed — the soul that cannot pass from the impure path to the pure. The creature vows to die in the flames of the Arctic (fire in ice — the unresolved opposition of the elements). Unlike the Ancient Mariner who returns transformed, the creature returns to the chaos from which it was made. This is the tragedy of the work that is entirely impure: the astral body dissolves back into the tattvas from which it was assembled, never having found its daimon. The 'darkness and distance' that swallow the creature are the anāśrita (the unsupported) — the dissolution without liberation.

gaps: The DeLacey subplot needs analysis as the pure path witnessed from outside; the creature's reading of Paradise Lost/Sorrows of Werter/Plutarch needs treatment as the tattvic curriculum; detailed page references needed

issues.title: Passages need refinement with specific page/letter references
issues.description: All passages currently summarize themes without precise textual citations.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. Six passages covering the creation as astral body, the creature's rejection as daimon-separation, the creature's speech as tattva-mirror, the ice hunt as Earth descent, Walton's frame as witness, and the creature's disappearance as threshold not crossed.
